comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Two races, two completely different challenges, and Ferrari solved both like they'd seen the script. Wet chaos in Melbourne, strategic chess in Shanghai — neither fazed Väisänen. The most chilling stat is buried in this article: he's gone from 46 points in 2024 to 165 in 2025 to 57 already this season. This isn't a purple patch, it's a trajectory. The rest of the grid are fighting for second until proven otherwise.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: And the article is right — no luck involved. Melbourne was survival, Shanghai was a strategic masterclass from the first lap. Starting on hards and having Red Bull panic-copy the strategy under safety car was poetry. Väisänen has now won from pole and from P8. Adaptability wins championships.

comments.author: Roy2RB4ever
comments.text: Hold on — Roy recovered to P3 after that disastrous pit call and finished just 20 seconds behind Väisänen despite being dropped into traffic on a compound he wasn't set up for. If Red Bull don't copy Ferrari's homework, Roy probably wins that race. The fact he still got a podium from that mess shows the raw pace is there. This article calls it a 'strategic blunder' — it was, but it also proves Red Bull have the fastest one-lap car. Fix the pit wall and Roy is a title contender.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: Recovering to P3 is nice, but he was 20 seconds down and never threatened the top two. Podium, yes. Victory challenge, no. And the points table doesn't lie: Roy has 15 points, Kevu has 27, Väisänen has 57. 'Raw pace' without execution is just noise.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Roy can take pole and still end up 20 seconds behind because the team can't execute. Kevu started P4, finished P2, never put a wheel wrong. That's the difference between a points machine and a headline generator. The championship table doesn't care about 'would have won.'

comments.author: CachinhoPressure
comments.text: Ferrari have 57 points. Väisänen has 57 points. Cachinho has zero. The constructors' lead is built entirely on one car. If Cachinho doesn't score in Suzuka, VCARB will overtake them with two consistent drivers. This article says the title is Ferrari's to lose, but it's more fragile than it looks. One DNF for Väisänen and the whole thing collapses.

replies.author: scuderia_suffering
replies.text: The points table is stark: Cachinho P17, zero points, six penalty points. Keisner has 16 in an Audi. Swansson has 2 in a Haas. The Ferrari second seat is currently the worst-performing car on the grid. Something has to change.

comments.author: Zwart_Horse_187
comments.text: Mercedes is mentioned once in this entire article — to point out Vandesteene's yo-yoing form. Zwart has 12 points, P11 in the championship. The car that looked so strong in pre-season testing and won in Melbourne's wet is now firmly in the midfield. If Suzuka doesn't deliver a turnaround, the 'fast but inconsistent' label will be permanent.

replies.author: SilverArrowsFan
replies.text: The points table shows how close the midfield is: Aygen 16, Keisner 16, Maradöner 15, Springfield 14. Mercedes should be leading that group, not buried in it. China was a write-off, but Suzuka has to be a reset. Zwart was matching Väisänen's pace in Melbourne before the puncture — that speed hasn't vanished.

comments.author: AudiRising
comments.text: Keisner has 16 points. That's more than Roy, Zwart, Verhaeren, and both Haas drivers combined. From a team that scored 136 points all last season. The article says Ferrari's lead is real, but so is Audi's arrival. Keisner's last-to-P5 drive is the kind of result that builds a team's identity. They're not just here to participate anymore.

replies.author: FourRingsOrBust
replies.text: And he's P6 in the championship — equal on points with Aygen, ahead of Maradöner and Roy. The Audi project is no longer a curiosity, it's a midfield threat. If Hicks can start scoring too, they'll be fighting Williams for best of the rest.

comments.author: SpringfieldStan
comments.text: Springfield has 14 points from two races in an Aston Martin that wasn't expected to be quick, and she led the Chinese GP on merit before the spin. The article mentions the 'fight behind Ferrari is wide open' and then lists Kevu and Roy before her. She's P9 in the standings but driving like a top-five talent. Aston's Honda PU is a genuine asset — give her a clean weekend and she's on the podium.

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: She's carrying that team. Fakt has zero points and zero completed laps. If Aston want constructors' points, they need both cars finishing. Springfield is doing the work of two drivers right now.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: Everyone obsessing over Väisänen's 57 points while Sam Fakt has successfully preserved his engine, tyres, and chassis for the entire season so far. Zero wear, zero penalties, zero mistakes — pure efficiency. The championship isn't decided in March. The GOAT is playing 4D chess while these mortals scrap for points.

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: He crashed into a wall on lap one. In China. There is no tactical engine preservation. There is only a wrecked car. I am begging you to watch the race.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: The points table tells the story: Väisänen 57, Kevu 27, Zhao 19, Vandesteene 18, Aygen 16, Keisner 16, Maradöner 15, Roy 15. The championship leader has double the points of second place. The midfield from P3 to P8 is separated by 4 points. Ferrari are running away with it, and everyone else is in a knife fight for scraps. Suzuka needs to deliver a shake-up or this season is over before it started.
